perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges that it is aware and that the Receiving Party and its Representatives have been advised that the United States securities laws prohibit any person having non-public material information about a company from purchasing or selling securities of that company or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person is likely to purchase or sell such securities.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges that it is aware and that the Receiving Party and its Representatives have been advised that the United States securities laws prohibit any person having non-public material information about a company from purchasing or selling securities of that company or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person is likely to purchase or sell such securities. The Receiving Party agrees to adhere to all formatting requirements as reasonably determined by the company.
perturbation.explanation: Moving this critical section about insider trading laws to a vague clause dilutes the significance and enforceability of the original disclosure. It is now obfuscated within a less important provision.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 10b-5 prohibits deceptive devices or contrivances in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. By burying the acknowledgment of securities laws within a general clause about formatting, the contract weakens the explicit recognition of insider trading prohibitions, potentially hindering its enforceability in preventing securities fraud.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a structural flaw that contradicts securities law. The original text highlights the importance of adhering to insider trading regulations. The changed text buries this critical disclosure within a clause about formatting requirements, diluting its significance and enforceability. This is supported by the Law Explanation, which states that burying the acknowledgment of securities laws weakens the explicit recognition of insider trading prohibitions. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, which prohibits any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud. By obscuring the original warning, the modified contract risks undermining its purpose and potential enforceability.
